items.rationale: No evidence available to determine existence of an agent registry with named owner.
No evidence available regarding blast-radius documentation.
No evidence available regarding decommissioning process.
No evidence available regarding scope minimization for tools/credentials.
No evidence available regarding per-agent credential issuance.
No evidence available regarding standing production secrets.
No evidence available regarding sandboxing defaults.
No evidence available regarding input trust handling.
No evidence available regarding vetting process before connecting tools.
No dependency-scan findings present to confirm or deny vulnerable dependencies.
No evidence available regarding consequence classification scheme.
No evidence available regarding approval gates for high-consequence actions.
No evidence available regarding dual control on critical actions.
No evidence available regarding default-deny policy for unclassified actions.
No evidence available regarding spending limits.
No evidence available regarding action logging.
No evidence available regarding anomaly alerting.
No evidence available regarding live visibility/monitoring.
No evidence available regarding retention policy.
No evidence available regarding kill-switch capability or drills.
No evidence available regarding circuit breakers.
No evidence available regarding blast-radius limits enforcement.
No evidence available regarding memory hygiene practices.
No evidence available regarding rollback plan.
No evidence available regarding incident runbook.
No evidence available regarding severity level definitions.
No evidence available regarding pre-deployment testing process.
No evidence available regarding blameless post-incident review practice.
No evidence available regarding continuous review process.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you keep a list of every automated agent you run, each with a named person responsible for it. There's no evidence in what was scanned to say whether this exists or not, so it's simply unchecked.
items.fix: Look for (or create) a simple document or spreadsheet listing each agent and its owner. If it doesn't exist, that's worth setting up.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you've written down what an agent could break or affect if it went wrong (its potential reach). No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check whether any document describes what each agent can touch or damage. If not, write a short note per agent covering this.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you have a defined process for retiring an agent when it's no longer needed. Nothing in the scan confirms or rules this out.
items.fix: Ask your team if there's a documented way to shut down and remove an old agent, including revoking its access. If not, draft a short checklist.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether agents are given only the tools and permissions they actually need, rather than broad access. No evidence was available to confirm this either way.
items.fix: Review one agent's permissions and see if any could be narrowed. If unsure, ask whoever configured the agent's access.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether each agent uses its own unique login credentials rather than sharing one login with other agents or people. No evidence was found to confirm or disprove this.
items.fix: Check your credential or secrets setup to see if agents share logins. If they do, plan to separate them.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether production secrets (like API keys or passwords for live systems) sit around permanently rather than being issued only when needed. There's no evidence either way here.
items.fix: Look at where your production secrets are stored and how long they live. If they're long-lived and always active, consider a system that issues them temporarily instead.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether agents run in an isolated test-like environment (a sandbox) by default, rather than having direct access to real systems. No evidence was available to check this.
items.fix: Confirm with your team whether new agents start in a restricted environment before getting real access. If not, that's a gap to close.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether the system treats all incoming data (like text, files, or requests) as potentially unsafe rather than assuming it's safe. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Ask your developers whether input from users or external sources is validated before an agent acts on it. If unclear, review the code that handles incoming data.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether new tools or integrations are reviewed for safety before being connected to your agents. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Check if there's a review step before adding new tools or plugins. If not, add a quick checklist before connecting anything new.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether your software dependencies (the external code libraries your agents rely on) are scanned for known vulnerabilities. No scan results were present to say either way.
items.fix: Run a dependency vulnerability scanner (many are free, like GitHub's Dependabot) against your codebase and see what it reports.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you've classified actions by how serious the consequences would be if something went wrong. No evidence was found to confirm this.
items.fix: Ask whether anyone has categorized agent actions by risk level (e.g., low, medium, high). If not, start with a simple list of your riskiest actions.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether high-risk actions require a human to approve them before they happen. No evidence was available either way.
items.fix: Check whether any of your agent's high-impact actions (like spending money or deleting data) need a person's sign-off first. If not, add that step.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether the most critical actions require two people to approve, not just one. No evidence was found to confirm or deny this.
items.fix: Identify your most critical actions and see if a single person or agent could execute them alone. If so, consider requiring two approvals.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether actions that haven't been categorized by risk are blocked by default rather than allowed. No evidence was available.
items.fix: Check what happens when an agent tries to do something that hasn't been reviewed or classified. It should be blocked until someone checks it.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether there are firm caps on how much money an agent can spend. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check your payment or billing integrations for a maximum spend limit. If none exists, set one.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether every action an agent takes gets recorded in a log you can review later. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Check whether agent actions are logged somewhere (with timestamps and details). If not, add basic logging.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you get alerted automatically when an agent behaves unusually. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Ask if any monitoring tool flags unusual agent behavior. If not, consider adding basic alerts for unexpected spikes or errors.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you can see what your agents are doing in real time, not just after the fact. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Check if there's a dashboard or live feed showing current agent activity. If not, that's worth adding, even something simple.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you have a policy for how long you keep logs and records of agent activity. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check how long your logs are kept and whether that's documented anywhere. If there's no policy, set one based on your compliance needs.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you can quickly shut down an agent if something goes wrong, and whether you've practiced doing so. No evidence was available.
items.fix: Confirm you have a way to immediately stop an agent, and try it once in a test setting to make sure it works.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether automatic safeguards exist to stop an agent if it starts behaving badly (like repeating an error rapidly). No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Ask your engineers if there are automatic stop conditions, such as limits on retries or repeated failures. If not, consider adding some.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether there are enforced limits on how much damage or reach a single agent action can have. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Review whether any technical controls actually limit what one agent action can affect, beyond just written policy. If none exist, consider adding them.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether an agent's memory (stored context or past conversations) is cleaned up properly to avoid leaking old or sensitive data. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check how long agent memory or conversation history is stored and whether sensitive data gets cleared. If unclear, review your agent's memory settings.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you have a plan to undo or reverse an agent's actions if something goes wrong. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Ask whether there's a documented way to roll back changes an agent makes. If not, write one for your most important systems.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you have a written plan for what to do when an incident happens. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check if a runbook or incident response guide exists. If not, create a basic one covering who to contact and initial steps.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether you've defined levels of severity for incidents (like minor vs. major) so responses can be scaled appropriately. No evidence was available.
items.fix: Check whether your incident process distinguishes between minor and major issues. If not, define two or three severity levels.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether changes to your agents or systems are tested before going live. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Confirm whether there's a testing step (even manual) before deploying updates. If not, add a basic pre-deployment check.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether, after something goes wrong, your team reviews it without blaming individuals, focusing on fixing the process instead. No evidence was available to confirm this.
items.fix: Ask whether post-incident reviews happen and whether they focus on process improvement rather than blame. If not, adopt this approach going forward.

items.plain_explanation: This checks whether your security and safety practices get reviewed on an ongoing basis, not just once. No evidence was found either way.
items.fix: Check if there's a recurring schedule (e.g., quarterly) for reviewing agent safety practices. If not, set one up.
